query: Florida Legislature special session 2026E budget negotiations House Senate conference report May 2026
answer:
The agent returned a comprehensive report. Let me compile the final structured output.

# Florida 2026E Special Session Budget Negotiations — Research Report

## Background

The 2026 regular legislative session ended **March 18, 2026** without passing a budget — an unusual and significant failure. Politico attributed the collapse to "distrust and dysfunction" within the GOP-controlled Legislature. Governor DeSantis confirmed on March 16 that a budget special session would be required. After a separate Special Session D in late April (covering redistricting, AI, and vaccine topics), House Speaker Daniel Perez and Senate President Ben Albritton agreed on a **$51.98 billion General Revenue framework** and announced an **18-day budget-only special session (Session E) running May 12–29, 2026**.

2026 has seen **five special session designations (A through E)** — a modern record.

## Total Budget Amounts

| Version | Total Appropriation (FY 2026-27) |
|---------|--------------------------------|
| **HB 5001-E (House)** | **$113,575,226,021 (~$113.6B)** |
| **SB 2500-E (Senate)** | **$115,025,780,033 (~$115.0B)** |
| **House-Senate Gap** | **~$1.45 billion** |
| Agreed General Revenue Framework | $51.98 billion |
| Governor DeSantis' Original Proposal | ~$117 billion |
| FY 2025-26 Enacted Budget (pre-veto) | ~$116.5 billion |

Both chambers proposed **slight reductions** from the prior year and are well below the Governor's request.

## Timeline of Negotiations

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| March 18 | Regular session ended without budget deal |
| April 23 | $51.98B GR framework agreed; May 12–29 session called |
| May 5 | HB 5001-E filed |
| **May 12** | Session E convened; House passed HB 5001-E **(98-8)**; Senate passed amended version **(32-0)**; Budget Conference Committee appointed |
| May 14 | $1.4B gap between chambers reported |
| May 18 | Housing funding priorities advanced in conference |
| May 19 | DeSantis signaled property tax reform may require yet another special session |
| May 20 | Conference negotiations ongoing across 7 subcommittees |
| **~May 25-26** | Effective deadline for conference agreement (72-hour cooling-off period required) |
| **May 29** | Scheduled adjournment |

## Conference Report Status (as of latest data, May 20, 2026)

**No final conference report had been filed or adopted as of May 20.** The bill was in active Budget Conference with seven subcommittees exchanging offers (House Offers 1 & 2, Senate Offers 1 & 2). A 72-hour cooling-off period is required between public release of the conference report and the final floor vote, meaning a deal needed to be struck by approximately **May 25–26** to allow votes before the May 29 deadline.

## Key Spending Disputes

| Issue | House | Senate | Gap |
|-------|-------|--------|-----|
| **Florida Forever (conservation)** | $25M | $75M | $50M (Gov. requested $115M) |
| **Rural & Family Lands** | $75M | $0 | Potential cross-trade |
| **Florida State Guard** | Proposed eliminating funding | — | Direct challenge to DeSantis priority |
| **Healthcare** | Contested | Contested | HMO cuts, nursing home rates, hospital funding |
| **Higher Education** | Lower funding | Higher funding | "Significant divide" reported |
| **State Employee Pay** | Contested | No health insurance cost hikes | Both support raises, differ on amounts |
| **Gas Tax Pause** | Under discussion | Under discussion | Driven by rising fuel prices |

## Conference Committee Structure

Seven subcommittees, all chaired by senators: Budget At Large (Sen. Hooper), Agriculture/Environment/General Government (Sen. Brodeur), Criminal and Civil Justice (Sen. Garcia), Health and Human Services (Sen. Trumbull), Higher Education (Sen. Harrell), Pre-K–12 Education (Sen. Burgess), Transportation/Tourism/Economic Development (Sen. DiCeglie).

## Governor's Role

- **Original proposal:** ~$117 billion — significantly above both chambers.
- **Property tax reform:** On May 19, signaled this would require yet another special session beyond Session E.
- **Line-item veto history:** DeSantis has consistently applied vetoes ranging from ~$800M to a record ~$3.1B (FY 2022-23). Any enacted budget will likely face significant vetoes.
- **Florida State Guard defunding** by the House is a direct challenge to a DeSantis signature initiative.

## Key Unknowns (as of May 22, 2026)

The most recent data available is from May 20, 2026. It remains unknown whether:
1. A conference agreement was reached between May 20–22.
2. A conference report has been filed.
3. The 72-hour cooling-off period has begun.
4. Final compromise figures on contested items have been set.

key_facts: The 2026 regular legislative session ended March 18, 2026 without passing a budget — an unusual failure attributed to 'distrust and dysfunction' within the GOP-controlled Legislature.

Special Session E (2026E) was convened May 12–29, 2026 as a budget-only special session, after House Speaker Daniel Perez and Senate President Ben Albritton agreed on a $51.98 billion General Revenue framework on April 23.
2026 has had five special session designations (A through E) — a modern record for a single year.
HB 5001-E (House version) totals ~$113.6 billion; SB 2500-E (Senate version) totals ~$115.0 billion, creating a gap of approximately $1.45 billion.
On May 12, the House passed HB 5001-E by a vote of 98-8 and the Senate passed its amended version 32-0; both chambers acceded to Budget Conference.
As of May 20, 2026, no final conference report had been filed or adopted. Negotiations were ongoing across 7 subcommittees.
A 72-hour cooling-off period is required between public release of the conference report and a final floor vote, setting an effective deal deadline of approximately May 25-26.

Key disputes include Florida Forever funding ($25M House vs $75M Senate), elimination of Florida State Guard funding (House proposal), higher education funding levels, healthcare spending, and state employee pay raises.
Governor DeSantis originally proposed a ~$117 billion budget — well above both chambers' positions.
On May 19, DeSantis signaled property tax reform would likely require yet another special session beyond Session E.
Both chambers' proposals represent slight reductions from the FY 2025-26 enacted budget of ~$116.5 billion.
The House proposed eliminating funding for the Florida State Guard, a signature DeSantis initiative, representing a direct challenge to gubernatorial priorities.
